meta.title: High Frequency, A (i)

source.publication: Section 1: Vocabulary, Word List I — High Frequency Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Every word, definition, synonym, and antonym transcribed faithfully from the source's opening entries; context sentences and the teaching layer are authored for CAT OS.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Word DNA milestone); WORD_DNA_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: The first twenty entries of the source's High Frequency list (abandon through accolade), taken in the source's own order rather than cherry-picked. One PDF column-wrap artifact under "abase" (a stray, ungrammatical fragment) was dropped as a scanning defect, not as an edit to content; its real definition and synonym/antonym pairs are intact. Owner review pending.

unit.label: High Frequency, A (i)
unit.mentor_note: Some words don't share a hidden pattern. They're simply worth meeting once, carefully, the way you'd meet someone whose name you want to remember.

members.context_sentence: The sailors had to abandon ship when it began to sink.

members.meaning: to lower in dignity; to humiliate
members.context_sentence: He refused to abase himself by begging for forgiveness in public.
The storm showed no sign of abating even after midnight.
Doctors often abbreviate long medical terms into short codes.
The king chose to abdicate the throne rather than change the law.

members.meaning: the giving up of control or authority
members.context_sentence: The abdication surprised the entire nation.

members.meaning: a straying away from what is normal
members.context_sentence: The scientist dismissed the strange reading as a minor aberration.

members.meaning: to assist, normally in a crime
members.context_sentence: The driver was charged with aiding and abetting the robbery.
She has always abhorred cruelty of any kind.
He could not abide the sound of chewing at the dinner table.

members.meaning: to promise or swear to give up
members.context_sentence: The soldier was made to abjure his allegiance to the old king.
Before entering the temple, worshippers perform a simple ablution.
The library only had an abridgement of the original three-volume novel.

members.meaning: to go away suddenly, especially to avoid arrest
members.context_sentence: The clerk absconded with the company's funds overnight.
The professor's lecture on quantum mechanics was so abstruse that half the class gave up taking notes.
Ticket sales for the film were abysmal in its opening week.

members.meaning: a bottomless pit; anything too deep to measure
members.context_sentence: Looking over the cliff, she felt she was staring into an abyss.
The committee finally acceded to the workers' demands.
The acclaimed director accepted the award to a standing ovation.
Winning the national award was the greatest accolade of her career.

discovery.notice_prompt: English is full of words that carry real weight. Read each one in its sentence, then guess before the reveal.

predict_options.text: To give up completely, or leave behind
To repair or fix something quickly

discovery.understand_note: Abandon carries more weight than a plain give up, whether it's a ship, a plan, or a belief. Once you notice a word like this, you start seeing it standing in for a dozen smaller, softer phrases.

applies.prompt: "The professor's lecture on quantum mechanics was so abstruse that half the class gave up taking notes." What does abstruse mean here?
"Winning the national award was the greatest accolade of her career." What does accolade mean here?

options.text: An expression of praise or honor
A large sum of prize money
